Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: Clearly 2005 and 2003 are long, 2002 moderate, 2001 and 2004 very short.

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Now identify performance issues: Cases with significantly longer durations. Probably 2005 and 2003, maybe 2002 is borderline. But we need to see what the normal should be. Low complexity cases (2001, 2004) finish within ~1.5h. Medium complexity (2002) took >1 day. High complexity (2003, 2005) took multiple days. That suggests Complexity level strongly correlates with longer durations.

Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: But there are differences within same complexity: both 2003 (High, Region A) and 2005 (High, Region B): 2005 took much longer (3.2 days vs 2.0 days). 2003 had 2 Request Additional Documents events, 2005 had 3. 2003 had 2 requests, 2005 had 3 requests.

Name: Hypothesis Generation - IND
Text: Manager_Ann for low/medium cases in both regions (2001 Manager_Ann for low A, 2002 Manager_Ann for medium B, 2004 Manager_Ann for low B). For high complexity cases, both used Manager_Bill. So manager assignment might be based on complexity: Manager_Bill for high complexity, Manager_Ann for low/medium. That suggests high complexity claims go to a different approver, possibly causing delays if Manager_Bill is slower?

Name: Inductive Reasoning - PE
Text: So root cause of longer durations for high complexity is likely the need for multiple document requests, which introduces waiting periods. The number of requests correlates with duration: 2003 had 2 requests, took 2 days; 2005 had 3 requests, took 3 days; 2002 (medium) had 1 request, took ~1 day. So the number of Additional Documents requests is directly proportional to days. So complexity leads to more requests, which extends process. That is a key root cause: high complexity claims require more back-and-forth, causing delays.

Name: Abductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Now consider Resource and Region differences: The 2005 (Region B) had more requests than 2003 (Region A) despite both being high complexity. Is that due to the adjuster? 2003: Adjuster_Mike (Region A) made 2 requests; 2005: Adjuster_Lisa (Region B) made 3 requests. Could be that Adjuster_Lisa tends to request more documents or that Region B claims are inherently more complex? But both are labeled High. So maybe the adjusters behavior or regional practices cause more requests... within high complexity, Region A with Adjuster_Mike had 2 requests, Region B with Adjuster_Lisa had 3 requests, resulting in longer duration. Could be due to adjuster diligence, but also maybe Region B has stricter documentation requirements.

Name: Validation - IND
Text: Possibly, Manager_Bill might be a bottleneck for approval after requests? For 2003, last request at 17:00, approve next day 16:00 (23h later). For 2005, last request at 15:00 on 04-03, approve at 10:00 next day (19h later), so approval delay somewhat similar. So the main driver is the number and timing of request activities. The time between submission and first request: 2003 request at 11:00 after evaluate at 09:40 (gap 1h20m). 2005 first request at 11:30 after evaluate at 09:50 (gap 1h40m). Similar.
